revision.text:
{{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[shōgun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
{{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[shōgun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
{{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[shōgun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
{{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[shōgun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
{{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[shōgun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
{{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[shōgun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
{{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
{{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''
