<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''[[:en:Elder (administrative title)|Elder]]'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''[[:en:Hyōjōsho|Hyōjōsho]]'' council, along with the ''[[:en:Metsuke|Ō-Metsuke]]'' and representatives of various ''[[:en:Bugyō|Bugyō]]'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a [[:en:supreme court|supreme court]], deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''[[:en:Tairō|Tairō]]'', Chamberlains, and others, including [[:en:Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu]], who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, [[:en:Hotta Masayoshi|Hotta Masayoshi]] served in 1857–58 after [[:en:Abe Masahiro|Abe Masahiro]] (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''{{ill|Elder (administrative title)|en|Elder (administrative title)|WD=Q1757970|lt=Elder}}'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''{{ill|Fudai daimyō|en|Fudai daimyō|WD=Q643640|lt=Fudai daimyō}}s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}}, the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''{{ill|Hyōjōsho|en|Hyōjōsho|WD=Q5962798|lt=Hyōjōsho}}'' council, along with the ''{{ill|Metsuke|en|Metsuke|WD=Q113752883|lt=Ō-Metsuke}}'' and representatives of various ''{{ill|Bugyō|en|Bugyō|WD=Q2535820|lt=Bugyō}}'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a {{ill|supreme court|en|supreme court|WD=Q190752|lt=supreme court}}, deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''{{ill|Tairō|en|Tairō|WD=Q956844|lt=Tairō}}'', Chamberlains, and others, including {{ill|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|en|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|WD=Q846911|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu}}, who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}.
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, {{ill|Hotta Masayoshi|en|Hotta Masayoshi|WD=Q910241|lt=Hotta Masayoshi}} served in 1857–58 after {{ill|Abe Masahiro|en|Abe Masahiro|WD=Q1070088|lt=Abe Masahiro}} (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''
===Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}=== *{{ill|Ōkubo Tadachika|en|Ōkubo Tadachika|WD=Q8081570|lt=Ōkubo Tadachika}} (大久保忠隣)(1593–1614) *{{ill|Ōkubo Nagayasu|en|Ōkubo Nagayasu|WD=Q133746|lt=Ōkubo Nagayasu}} (大久保長安)(1600–1613) *{{ill|Sakakibara Yasumasa|en|Sakakibara Yasumasa|WD=Q1062738|lt=Sakakibara Yasumasa}} (榊原康政)(1600–1606) *{{ill|Honda Masanobu|en|Honda Masanobu|WD=Q845447|lt=Honda Masanobu}} (本多正信)(1600–1615) *[[Naruse Masanari]] (成瀬正成)(1600–1616) *[[Andō Naotsugu]] (安藤直次)(1600–1616) *{{ill|Honda Masazumi|en|Honda Masazumi|WD=Q1058809|lt=Honda Masazumi}} (本多正純)(1600–1622) *[[Naitō Kiyonari]] (内藤清成)(1601–1606) *{{ill|Aoyama Tadanari|en|Aoyama Tadanari|WD=Q4778834|lt=Aoyama Tadanari}} (青山忠成)(1601–1606)

===Under {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}}=== *{{ill|Abe Masatsugu|en|Abe Masatsugu|WD=Q4666427|lt=Abe Masatsugu}} (阿部正次)(1623–1626) *{{ill|Inaba Masakatsu|en|Inaba Masakatsu|WD=Q6013267|lt=Inaba Masakatsu}} (稲葉正勝)(1623–1634) *[[Naitō Tadashige]] (内藤忠重)(1623–1633) *{{ill|Sakai Tadakatsu|en|Sakai Tadakatsu|WD=Q7402768|lt=Sakai Tadakatsu}} (酒井忠勝)(1624–1638) *[[Morikawa Shigetoshi]] (森川重俊)(1628–1632) *[[Aoyama Yukinari]] (青山幸成)(1628–1633) *{{ill|Matsudaira Nobutsuna|en|Matsudaira Nobutsuna|WD=Q6788056|lt=Matsudaira Nobutsuna}} (松平信綱)(1632–1662) *{{ill|Abe Tadaaki|en|Abe Tadaaki|WD=Q4666449|lt=Abe Tadaaki}} (阿部忠秋)(1633–1666) *{{ill|Hotta Masamori|en|Hotta Masamori|WD=Q1631219|lt=Hotta Masamori}} (堀田正盛)(1635–1651) *[[Abe Shigetsugu]] (阿部重次)(1638–1651) *{{ill|Matsudaira Norinaga|en|Matsudaira Norinaga|WD=Q6788057|lt=Matsudaira Norinaga}} (松平乗寿)(1642–1654)
===Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsuna|en|Tokugawa Ietsuna|WD=Q323422|lt=Tokugawa Ietsuna}}=== *{{ill|Sakai Tadakiyo|en|Sakai Tadakiyo|WD=Q7402769|lt=Sakai Tadakiyo}} (酒井忠清)(1653–1666) *{{ill|Inaba Masanori|en|Inaba Masanori|WD=Q6013288|lt=Inaba Masanori}} (稲葉正則)(1657–1681) *[[Kuze Hiroyuki]] (久世広之)(1663–1679) *{{ill|Itakura Shigenori|en|Itakura Shigenori|WD=Q11532529|lt=Itakura Shigenori}} (板倉重矩)(1665–1668, 1670–1673) *[[Tsuchiya Kazunao]] (土屋数直)(1665–1679) *{{ill|Abe Masayoshi|en|Abe Masayoshi|WD=Q3331296|lt=Abe Masayoshi}} (阿部正能)(1673–1676) *{{ill|Ōkubo Tadatomo|en|Ōkubo Tadatomo|WD=Q8081578|lt=Ōkubo Tadatomo}} (大久保忠朝)(1677–1698) *{{ill|Hotta Masatoshi|en|Hotta Masatoshi|WD=Q936047|lt=Hotta Masatoshi}} (堀田正俊)(1679–1681) *[[:en:Doi Toshifusa|Doi Toshifusa]] (土井利房)(1679–1681) *[[Itakura Shigetane]] (板倉重種)(1680–1681)
===Under [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]]=== *{{ill|Toda Tadamasa|en|Toda Tadamasa|WD=Q3530227|lt=Toda Tadamasa}} (戸田忠昌)(1681–1699) *[[Abe Masatake]] (阿部正武)(1681–1704) *[[Matsudaira Nobuyuki]] (松平信之)(1685–1686) *{{ill|Tsuchiya Masanao|en|Tsuchiya Masanao|WD=Q3181445|lt=Tsuchiya Masanao}} (土屋政直)(1687–1718) *{{ill|Ogasawara Nagashige|en|Ogasawara Nagashige|WD=Q7079832|lt=Ogasawara Nagashige}} (小笠原長重)(1697–1705, 1709–1710) *[[Akimoto Takatomo]] (秋元喬知)(1699–1707) *{{ill|Inaba Masamichi|en|Inaba Masamichi|WD=Q6013274|lt=Inaba Masamichi}} (稲葉正往)(1701–1707) *[[Honda Masanaga]] (本多正永)(1704–1711) *{{ill|Ōkubo Tadamasu|en|Ōkubo Tadamasu|WD=Q11431969|lt=Ōkubo Tadamasu}} (大久保忠増)(1705–1713) *[[Inoue Masamine]] (井上正岑)(1705–1722)

(2006). ''Secret Memoirs of the Shoguns: Isaac Titsingh and Japan, 1779–1822,'' p. 242n91. Also known as "Honda Tadayoshi"</ref> *{{ill|Toki Yoritoshi|en|Toki Yoritoshi|WD=Q7813576|lt=Toki Yoritoshi}} (土岐頼稔)(1742–1744) *{{ill|Sakai Tadazumi|en|Sakai Tadazumi|WD=Q8515243|lt=Sakai Tadazumi}} (酒井忠恭)(1744–1749) *{{ill|Matsudaira Norikata|en|Matsudaira Norikata|WD=Q6788054|lt=Matsudaira Norikata}} (松平乗賢)(1745–1746) *[[Hotta Masasuke]] (堀田正亮)(1745–1761)

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''{{ill|Elder (administrative title)|en|Elder (administrative title)|WD=Q1757970|lt=Elder}}'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''{{ill|Fudai daimyō|en|Fudai daimyō|WD=Q643640|lt=Fudai daimyō}}s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}}, the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''{{ill|Hyōjōsho|en|Hyōjōsho|WD=Q5962798|lt=Hyōjōsho}}'' council, along with the ''{{ill|Metsuke|en|Metsuke|WD=Q113752883|lt=Ō-Metsuke}}'' and representatives of various ''{{ill|Bugyō|en|Bugyō|WD=Q2535820|lt=Bugyō}}'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a {{ill|supreme court|en|supreme court|WD=Q190752|lt=supreme court}}, deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''{{ill|Tairō|en|Tairō|WD=Q956844|lt=Tairō}}'', Chamberlains, and others, including {{ill|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|en|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|WD=Q846911|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu}}, who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}.
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, {{ill|Hotta Masayoshi|en|Hotta Masayoshi|WD=Q910241|lt=Hotta Masayoshi}} served in 1857–58 after {{ill|Abe Masahiro|en|Abe Masahiro|WD=Q1070088|lt=Abe Masahiro}} (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''
===Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}=== *{{ill|Ōkubo Tadachika|en|Ōkubo Tadachika|WD=Q8081570|lt=Ōkubo Tadachika}} (大久保忠隣)(1593–1614) *{{ill|Ōkubo Nagayasu|en|Ōkubo Nagayasu|WD=Q133746|lt=Ōkubo Nagayasu}} (大久保長安)(1600–1613) *{{ill|Sakakibara Yasumasa|en|Sakakibara Yasumasa|WD=Q1062738|lt=Sakakibara Yasumasa}} (榊原康政)(1600–1606) *{{ill|Honda Masanobu|en|Honda Masanobu|WD=Q845447|lt=Honda Masanobu}} (本多正信)(1600–1615) *[[Naruse Masanari]] (成瀬正成)(1600–1616) *[[Andō Naotsugu]] (安藤直次)(1600–1616) *{{ill|Honda Masazumi|en|Honda Masazumi|WD=Q1058809|lt=Honda Masazumi}} (本多正純)(1600–1622) *[[Naitō Kiyonari]] (内藤清成)(1601–1606) *{{ill|Aoyama Tadanari|en|Aoyama Tadanari|WD=Q4778834|lt=Aoyama Tadanari}} (青山忠成)(1601–1606)
===Under {{ill|Tokugawa Hidetada|en|Tokugawa Hidetada|WD=Q314464|lt=Tokugawa Hidetada}}=== *[[Aoyama Narishige]] (青山成重)(1608–1613) *{{ill|Sakai Tadatoshi|en|Sakai Tadatoshi|WD=Q11644106|lt=Sakai Tadatoshi}} (酒井忠利)(1609–1627) *{{ill|Sakai Tadayo|en|Sakai Tadayo|WD=Q1065113|lt=Sakai Tadayo}} (酒井忠世)(1610–1634) *{{ill|Doi Toshikatsu|en|Doi Toshikatsu|WD=Q1042428|lt=Doi Toshikatsu}} (土井利勝)(1610–1638) *[[Andō Shigenobu]] (安藤重信)(1611–1621) *[[Naitō Kiyotsugu]] (内藤清次)(1616–1617) *{{ill|Aoyama Tadatoshi|en|Aoyama Tadatoshi|WD=Q4778838|lt=Aoyama Tadatoshi}} (青山忠俊)(1616–1623) *[[Inoue Masanari]] (井上正就)(1617–1628) *{{ill|Nagai Naomasa|en|Nagai Naomasa|WD=Q6958349|lt=Nagai Naomasa}} (永井尚政)(1622–1633)
===Under {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}}=== *{{ill|Abe Masatsugu|en|Abe Masatsugu|WD=Q4666427|lt=Abe Masatsugu}} (阿部正次)(1623–1626) *{{ill|Inaba Masakatsu|en|Inaba Masakatsu|WD=Q6013267|lt=Inaba Masakatsu}} (稲葉正勝)(1623–1634) *[[Naitō Tadashige]] (内藤忠重)(1623–1633) *{{ill|Sakai Tadakatsu|en|Sakai Tadakatsu|WD=Q7402768|lt=Sakai Tadakatsu}} (酒井忠勝)(1624–1638) *[[Morikawa Shigetoshi]] (森川重俊)(1628–1632) *[[Aoyama Yukinari]] (青山幸成)(1628–1633) *{{ill|Matsudaira Nobutsuna|en|Matsudaira Nobutsuna|WD=Q6788056|lt=Matsudaira Nobutsuna}} (松平信綱)(1632–1662) *{{ill|Abe Tadaaki|en|Abe Tadaaki|WD=Q4666449|lt=Abe Tadaaki}} (阿部忠秋)(1633–1666) *{{ill|Hotta Masamori|en|Hotta Masamori|WD=Q1631219|lt=Hotta Masamori}} (堀田正盛)(1635–1651) *[[Abe Shigetsugu]] (阿部重次)(1638–1651) *{{ill|Matsudaira Norinaga|en|Matsudaira Norinaga|WD=Q6788057|lt=Matsudaira Norinaga}} (松平乗寿)(1642–1654)
===Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsuna|en|Tokugawa Ietsuna|WD=Q323422|lt=Tokugawa Ietsuna}}=== *{{ill|Sakai Tadakiyo|en|Sakai Tadakiyo|WD=Q7402769|lt=Sakai Tadakiyo}} (酒井忠清)(1653–1666) *{{ill|Inaba Masanori|en|Inaba Masanori|WD=Q6013288|lt=Inaba Masanori}} (稲葉正則)(1657–1681) *[[Kuze Hiroyuki]] (久世広之)(1663–1679) *{{ill|Itakura Shigenori|en|Itakura Shigenori|WD=Q11532529|lt=Itakura Shigenori}} (板倉重矩)(1665–1668, 1670–1673) *[[Tsuchiya Kazunao]] (土屋数直)(1665–1679) *{{ill|Abe Masayoshi|en|Abe Masayoshi|WD=Q3331296|lt=Abe Masayoshi}} (阿部正能)(1673–1676) *{{ill|Ōkubo Tadatomo|en|Ōkubo Tadatomo|WD=Q8081578|lt=Ōkubo Tadatomo}} (大久保忠朝)(1677–1698) *{{ill|Hotta Masatoshi|en|Hotta Masatoshi|WD=Q936047|lt=Hotta Masatoshi}} (堀田正俊)(1679–1681) *[[:en:Doi Toshifusa|Doi Toshifusa]] (土井利房)(1679–1681) *[[Itakura Shigetane]] (板倉重種)(1680–1681)

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''{{ill|Elder (administrative title)|en|Elder (administrative title)|WD=Q1757970|lt=Elder}}'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''{{ill|Fudai daimyō|en|Fudai daimyō|WD=Q643640|lt=Fudai daimyō}}s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}}, the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''{{ill|Hyōjōsho|en|Hyōjōsho|WD=Q5962798|lt=Hyōjōsho}}'' council, along with the ''{{ill|Metsuke|en|Metsuke|WD=Q113752883|lt=Ō-Metsuke}}'' and representatives of various ''{{ill|Bugyō|en|Bugyō|WD=Q2535820|lt=Bugyō}}'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a {{ill|supreme court|en|supreme court|WD=Q190752|lt=supreme court}}, deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''{{ill|Tairō|en|Tairō|WD=Q956844|lt=Tairō}}'', Chamberlains, and others, including {{ill|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|en|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|WD=Q846911|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu}}, who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}.
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, {{ill|Hotta Masayoshi|en|Hotta Masayoshi|WD=Q910241|lt=Hotta Masayoshi}} served in 1857–58 after {{ill|Abe Masahiro|en|Abe Masahiro|WD=Q1070088|lt=Abe Masahiro}} (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''
===Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}=== *{{ill|Ōkubo Tadachika|en|Ōkubo Tadachika|WD=Q8081570|lt=Ōkubo Tadachika}} (大久保忠隣)(1593–1614) *{{ill|Ōkubo Nagayasu|en|Ōkubo Nagayasu|WD=Q133746|lt=Ōkubo Nagayasu}} (大久保長安)(1600–1613) *{{ill|Sakakibara Yasumasa|en|Sakakibara Yasumasa|WD=Q1062738|lt=Sakakibara Yasumasa}} (榊原康政)(1600–1606) *{{ill|Honda Masanobu|en|Honda Masanobu|WD=Q845447|lt=Honda Masanobu}} (本多正信)(1600–1615) *[[Naruse Masanari]] (成瀬正成)(1600–1616) *[[Andō Naotsugu]] (安藤直次)(1600–1616) *{{ill|Honda Masazumi|en|Honda Masazumi|WD=Q1058809|lt=Honda Masazumi}} (本多正純)(1600–1622) *[[Naitō Kiyonari]] (内藤清成)(1601–1606) *{{ill|Aoyama Tadanari|en|Aoyama Tadanari|WD=Q4778834|lt=Aoyama Tadanari}} (青山忠成)(1601–1606)

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Rōjū]][[cs:Ródžú]][[de:Rōjū]][[en:Rōjū]][[es:Rōjū]][[eu:Rōjū]][[fa:روجو]][[fr:Rōjū]][[it:Rōjū]][[ja:老中]][[ko:노중]][[pt:Rōjū]][[ru:Родзю]][[th:โรจู]][[tr:Rōjū]][[uk:Родзю]][[vi:Rōjū]][[zh:老中]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196812]] Wikidata name: Rōjū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|One of the highest-Ranking posts of Tokugawa Shogunate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} The {{nihongo|'''''Rōjū'''''|老中|}}, usually translated as ''{{ill|Elder (administrative title)|en|Elder (administrative title)|WD=Q1757970|lt=Elder}}'', was one of the highest-Ranking government posts under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The term refers either to individual Elders, or to the Council of Elders as a whole; under the first two ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s'', there were only two ''Rōjū''. The number was then increased to five, and later reduced to four. The ''Rōjū'' were usually appointed from the Ranks of the ''{{ill|Fudai daimyō|en|Fudai daimyō|WD=Q643640|lt=Fudai daimyō}}s'' with domains of between 25,000 and 50,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}''.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book|last=Beasley |first=William G |year=1972 |title=The Meiji Restoration |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0804708150 |page=18}}</ref>
==Duties==
The ''Rōjū'' had a number of responsibilities, most clearly delineated in the 1634 ordinance that reorganized the government and created a number of new posts:
:#Relations with the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}}, the Court, and the Prince-Abbots. :#Supervision of those ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' who controlled lands worth at least 10,000 ''koku''. :#Managing the forms taken by official documents in official communications. :#Supervision of the internal affairs of the Shogun's domains. :#Coinage, public works, and enfiefment. :#Governmental relations and supervision of monk monasteries and shrines. :#Compilation of maps, charts, and other government records.
The ''Rōjū'' served not simultaneously, but in rotation, each serving the Shogun for a month at a time, communicating with the Shogun through a chamberlain, called ''Soba-yōnin''. However, the ''Rōjū'' also served as members of the ''{{ill|Hyōjōsho|en|Hyōjōsho|WD=Q5962798|lt=Hyōjōsho}}'' council, along with the ''{{ill|Metsuke|en|Metsuke|WD=Q113752883|lt=Ō-Metsuke}}'' and representatives of various ''{{ill|Bugyō|en|Bugyō|WD=Q2535820|lt=Bugyō}}'' (Commissions or Departments). As part of the ''Hyōjōsho'', the ''Rōjū'' sometimes served a role similar to that of a {{ill|supreme court|en|supreme court|WD=Q190752|lt=supreme court}}, deciding succession disputes and other such disputed matters of state and its vassals.
Under the reign of [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1680–1709) the ''Rōjū'' lost nearly all their power, as the Shogun began to work more closely with the ''{{ill|Tairō|en|Tairō|WD=Q956844|lt=Tairō}}'', Chamberlains, and others, including {{ill|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|en|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|WD=Q846911|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu}}, who held the power of a ''Tairō'', but not the title. The ''Rōjū'' became little more than messengers, going through the motions of their proper roles as intermediaries between the Shogun and other offices, but not being able to exercise any power to change or decide policy. As [[Arai Hakuseki]], a major Confucian poet and politician of the time wrote, "All the Rōjū did was to pass on his [Yoshiyasu's]instructions" (Sansom 141). Even after Tsunayoshi's death, the ''Rōjū'' did not regain their former power. They continued to exist, however, as a government post and a council with, officially if not in fact, all the powers and responsibilities they originally held, through the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}.
==List of ''Rōjū''== ''Each office-holder is listed once. Some may have served under multiple ''shōguns'', and as a result of multiple terms, the list may not fully accurate reflect the order in which the office was held. For example, {{ill|Hotta Masayoshi|en|Hotta Masayoshi|WD=Q910241|lt=Hotta Masayoshi}} served in 1857–58 after {{ill|Abe Masahiro|en|Abe Masahiro|WD=Q1070088|lt=Abe Masahiro}} (1843–57), but also served earlier, and is listed earlier; he is not also listed after Abe.''
